claim: The zodiac and label-heavy sections (zodiac entropy 3.7149 bits, astronomical 3.7471 bits) use a homophonic substitution scheme in which common plaintext letters map to multiple glyphs, suppressing entropy relative to the herbal and text-only sections, while the text-only section (entropy 3.9016 bits) uses a simpler monoalphabetic or unenciphered encoding.
evidence_for: Zodiac section has lowest entropy (3.7149) and consists primarily of short labels, the classic context for homophones to suppress frequency peaks
Text-only section has highest entropy (3.9016 bits), closest to natural Latin/Italian entropy, consistent with less aggressive homophonic expansion
Top-10 word list is dominated by short tokens (ol, ar, or, y) typical of label vocabularies with limited semantic variety
evidence_against: Homophonic substitution should push entropy toward the maximum for the glyph set; lower entropy in label sections is more consistent with reduced vocabulary, not homophones
Only 25 unique glyphs are recorded; a true homophonic cipher on Latin (23-letter alphabet) would require more symbols unless many-to-one mapping is very coarse
Word entropy 10.4508 bits is very high, inconsistent with the vocabulary compression expected from homophones
test: For each section, compute the index of coincidence (IC) at the glyph level and compare to expected IC for monoalphabetic substitution (~0.065 for Latin) vs. homophonic/polyalphabetic (~0.038). Also compute type-token ratio by section.
test_metric: Per-section index of coincidence; type-token ratio; Pearson correlation between section entropy and section IC.
test_threshold: Confirmation of homophonic in zodiac/astronomical: IC < 0.050 for those sections while text-only IC > 0.055. Denial: IC values are statistically uniform across all sections (ANOVA p > 0.05).

reasoning: Establishing that low-entropy sections use homophones while text-only does not would create a ranked decipherment order starting with the text-only section, providing the clearest signal.
